key_insight: Geometrically necessary boundaries (GNBs) are the main carriers of long-range residual elastic strain in cold-rolled Fe-3%Si, while incidental dislocation boundary (IDB) cells form inside strain-similar GNB-delimited subdomains.
engineering_relevance: This clarifies how plastic substructure stores strain in electrical steels, informing recovery and recrystallization models for processing–property control.
method: Used dark-field X-ray microscopy (DFXM) for simultaneous 3D, bulk-sensitive mapping of intragranular misorientation and residual elastic strain.
Non-destructively resolved GNBs and IDB cell structures within a single grain of 50% cold-rolled Fe-3%Si.
Correlated segmented plastic substructures with spatial d-spacing/strain variations across the grain volume.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports direct 3D experimental correlation showing GNBs separate subdomains with distinct mean d-spacing, while IDB cells form within regions of comparatively similar elastic strain.
watch_item: The conclusions are based on a single-grain, intragranular observation, so broader generalization to other steels, textures, or deformation states should be validated experimentally.
paper_title: Geometrically necessary boundaries accommodate the residual elastic strain in cold-rolled Fe-3%Si
